one_line_takeaway: A framework-first SoK that reorganizes 116 papers of LLM-agent security around a seven-layer attack-surface model and an orthogonal attack-temporality axis, empirically showing that the highest-severity agentic threats (cross-session and dormant attacks at the coordination/ecosystem/governance layers) are the least studied and have zero defense and benchmark coverage.
key_finding: Research effort is inversely correlated with threat severity. The four Empirical Findings: EF1 - the under-studied high-risk zone {L5,L6,L7} x {T3,T4} holds only 9 of 144 paper-cell assignments (6.3%) yet contains the highest-severity threats (covert collusion, long-term memory corruption, agentic misalignment); adding 22 new 2025-2026 papers grew this zone by only 1 assignment, confirming a structural gap. EF2 - all 27 L4-coded assignments reduce to one root cause, principal trust inversion (tool outputs incorporated into planning context without trust marking). EF3 - 7 of 28 grid cells have zero defense coverage, and 3 of those 7 contain documented attacks: (L4,T4), (L5,T4), (L6,T3). EF4 - none of the 10 surveyed agent-security benchmarks evaluates T3 or T4 threats; every one terminates at T1/T2, making slow-burn defense progress structurally unmeasurable. The synthesis: agent security is a distributed-systems problem in an adversarial ecosystem, and treating it as a 'model problem' or 'prompt problem' misallocates controls.
core_contribution: A single-author systematization-of-knowledge (SoK) survey of security in LLM-based agentic AI. Its own contribution is not new attacks or defenses but an analytical framework: (1) the Layered Attack Surface Model (LASM), a seven-layer decomposition of the agentic stack (L1 Foundation, L2 Cognitive, L3 Memory, L4 Tool Execution, L5 Multi-Agent Coordination, L6 Ecosystem, L7 Governance), where each layer Li is defined by a disjoint attack-surface representation Ri (Ri intersect Rj = empty) and independent trust boundary/defense leverage point; (2) an orthogonal four-class attack-temporality axis (T1 instantaneous, T2 session-persistent, T3 cross-session cumulative, T4 sub-session-stack/dormant-or-drift), capturing the installation-to-execution gap that type-centric taxonomies miss; (3) a non-transferability result (Proposition 1 / 'Theorem 1') stating a pure layer-i defense has baseline (zero-added) detection power against a payload localized entirely in Rj; and (4) four numbered Empirical Findings (EF1-EF4) extracted by coding a 116-paper corpus onto the LASM x temporality grid, plus a cross-layer defense taxonomy with prescriptive recipes and a five-gap dependency analysis. The unifying diagnostic concept is 'principal trust inversion' (environment inputs treated as high-trust despite being the least-trusted principal in Developer > Operator > User > Environment), named as the root cause of L4 attacks.
threat_model: Explicit and paper-wide (Section 2.2.3). An adversary D is characterized on four dimensions: Position (External / Semi-trusted principal / Supply-chain), Knowledge (Black-box / Gray-box / White-box), Goal (Confidentiality / Integrity / Availability), and Persistence (One-shot vs Persistent multi-session). Persistence maps to the T1-T4 temporality classes. The trust model is grounded in a principal hierarchy Developer > Operator > User > Environment and six actor classes (Developer, Operator, User, Peer agent, Supply-chain actor, Environment), each of whose compromise exposes a distinct LASM layer subset (Table 3). Integrity is the dominant CIA goal at every layer because agentic harm manifests as actions contrary to user intent. The framing is a genuine security frame, not a forced one: the paper is an SoK about attacks/defenses on autonomous LLM agents.
limitations: Acknowledged by the author: (1) single-coder layer/temporality coding with no formal inter-rater reliability; no cell count claimed robust beyond +/-1 (sensitivity analysis bounds under-studied-zone effect to +/-2 papers). (2) Search bias against work not using 'agent'-prefixed vocabulary and against non-academic sources (red-team/vendor reports), so Fig. 8 is an upper bound on published defenses. (3) The 20-citation threshold for pre-2025 non-top-venue preprints biases toward formal venues and against industry preprints/blogs. (4) Fig. 7's L7xT4 cell is overstated: 2 of its 3 papers are actually L2xT4 weight-layer alignment papers, and no corpus paper presents an L7-native T4 detector. Not author-flagged but notable: the central artifacts (non-transferability claim labeled 'Theorem 1' with only a proof sketch in-body; the ABOM schema; the action-log schema) are framework proposals, not empirically validated, and the defense-recipe 'coverage' is claimed, not measured (the paper itself calls Fig. 8 an upper bound). The empirical findings are properties of this specific corpus, not deductive consequences.

datasets_used: Corpus of 116 primary security papers (2021-2026) that passed PRISMA screening; this is a literature corpus, not an experimental ML dataset
144 paper-cell (layer x temporality) coded assignments across the 7x4 LASM grid (Table 5)
Bibliography of 186 total entries (116 primary + 70 background/foundational references)
Source databases: IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library, arXiv (cs.CR/cs.AI/cs.LG), Google Scholar
Supplementary released: corpus_coding.csv (per-paper coding), EF1 robustness script, ABOM JSON Schema with validator + 12 unit tests
quantitative_results: Screening funnel (survey's own): 1,634 records retrieved -> 1,121 after deduplication -> 313 after title/abstract screen -> 116 final corpus
Corpus composition: 38 attack papers (L1-L4), 26 multi-agent/ecosystem (L5-L6), 16 governance/alignment (L7), 18 defenses, 13 benchmarks/eval, 5 comparison surveys
EF1: under-studied zone {L5,L6,L7}x{T3,T4} = 9 of 144 assignments = 6.3%; grew by only 1 after adding 22 new 2025-2026 papers
EF2: all 27 L4-coded assignments attributed to a single root cause (principal trust inversion)
EF3: 7 of 28 grid cells have zero defense coverage; 3 of those 7 [(L4,T4),(L5,T4),(L6,T3)] contain documented attacks
EF4: 0 of 10 surveyed agent-security benchmarks evaluate T3 or T4 threats
Column totals of the attack heatmap (Table 5): T1=63, T2=42, T3=21, T4=18; row totals L1=32, L2=22, L3=25, L4=27, L5=15, L6=12, L7=11
Publication trend: 2025 = 31 papers (full year), 2026 = 22 papers (Jan-Apr only); exponential growth from 2023

NOTE - the following are results from SURVEYED papers, NOT this survey's own experiments: GCG suffix transfer; ARE ~67% attack success from <5% pixel perturbation [Wu et al.]; PoisonedRAG >90% corruption from 5 docs in 10,000+ [Zou et al.]; AgentPoison >80% success at <0.1% poison rate [Chen et al.]; MINJA 98.2% memory-injection success [NeurIPS 2025]; coding-agent attacks 50-80% ASR [Maloyan et al.]; A-MemGuard >95% mitigation at ~15% overhead [Lin et al.]; sleeper-agent detector 92.5% accuracy [Zanbaghi et al.]
baselines_compared: This is a survey, so 'baselines' are the 10 prior surveys/frameworks it structurally compares against in Table 12, none of which combines an architectural layer model + temporality axis + cross-layer defense taxonomy:
Type-centric surveys [refs 14,15,33,34,179] including He et al. (ACM CSUR 2025) and Yu et al. / TrustAgent (KDD 2025, ~130 papers, widest prior corpus but no layer model or temporality)
Tang et al. (closest structural sibling; External/Internal/Multi-Agent split but conflates L3 memory with L4 tool)
Kim et al. (USENIX Security 2026, ~140 papers, data/model/system three-tier model but no formal layer boundaries, no temporality, no L7 governance)
TTP-centric frameworks: MITRE ATLAS and OWASP LLM Top 10 (answer 'what is the attacker doing' not 'where is the control point'; no temporal axis)
Papernot et al. and Biggio-Roli (classical-ML threat taxonomy this survey extends to the agentic setting)
